content.plain_english: This week in Year 5 maths, your child is working on Position and direction. The learning focuses on: identify, describe and represent the position of a shape following a reflection or translation, using appropriate language and know that the shape has not changed. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children handle, sort, and classify 2-D and 3-D shapes, drawing and describing their properties. They use protractors, mirrors, and squared paper to explore angles, symmetry, and position. Explaining what they notice in precise mathematical language is a key part of every lesson.
content.home_activity: Look for real-life examples around the house — a map showing distances, a measuring jug with ml and pints, a food label in grams and kg. Ask your child to read the measurements and convert between them. Road signs in miles are another good context.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to identify, describe and represent the position of a shape following a reflection or translation, using appropriate language?
Was it easy or tricky to know that the shape has not changed? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: A pair of numbers giving the exact position of a point on a grid — e.g. (3, 4).
The horizontal line on a coordinate grid.
The vertical line on a coordinate grid.
Sliding a shape to a new position without rotating or flipping it.
Flipping a shape over a mirror line.

example_questions: Write 0.375 as a fraction in its simplest form.
content.times_table_tip: By Year 5 children should know all their times tables with speed and confidence. Ask quick-fire questions at dinner — mixing tables so they recall facts rather than just reciting sequences. Can they answer in under 3 seconds?
